activity_metadata.objective: Students review key points from "Teenagers" by Pat Mora to show literal comprehension of the text.
activity_metadata.alignment_long: This activity replaces the Quiz and Think question segment of the Teenagers First Read activity. It offers a scaffolded sequence that guides students through demonstrating comprehension by responding to questions using evidence from the text.
activity_metadata.excerpt:
Teenagers by Pat Mora

(1) One day they disappear

       into their rooms.

       Doors and lips shut

       and we become strangers

(5) in our own home.

       I pace the hall, hear whispers,

       a code I knew but can't remember,

       mouthed by mouths I taught to speak.

       Years later the door opens.

(10) I see faces I once held,

       open as sunflowers in my hands. I see

       familiar skin now stretched on long bodies

       that move past me

       glowing almost like pearls.
exit_ticket: Write two or three sentences that explain what happens to the “strangers” when “years later, the door opens”?

questions_map.question:
**Voice** is the way an author uses word choice, tone, and speech patterns to show the personality of a speaker, narrator, or character. Voice gives the sense that a real person is talking to the reader or to other characters. 

Whose voice is telling us about the teenagers?
In lines 4 and 5, the speaker says that ”we become strangers in our own home.” Who are the strangers in this home?
Re-read lines 6-8. Which of the following inferences is best supported by these lines?
Look at lines 6-8 again. What does the word “code” mean in this context?
Re-read lines 9-14. These lines primarily suggest that _____.
Write two or three sentences that explain what happens to the “strangers” when “years later, the door opens”?

USER_10.question: Write two or three sentences that explain what happens to the “strangers” when “years later, the door opens”?

responses.content: They were all grown up and were not kids anymore!
They have grown taller and more beautiful than ever
I don’t understand
When the door opens the strangers see each other again and notice how much they have changed. They remember the faces they once knew and feel a connection again.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response explains that when the door opens, the strangers see each other again, notice how much they have changed, remember the faces they once knew, and feel a connection again. This matches the key scene elements: the parents/speaker (the 'strangers') reconnect with their grown children, recognize them, and experience a positive reunion. The answer is descriptive, text-specific, and aligns with the poem's meaning. There is no contradiction or missing key element.

USER_12.question: In lines 4 and 5, the speaker says that ”we become strangers in our own home.” Who are the strangers in this home?

responses.content: The voice that is telling us about the teenagers is the voice of the narrator.
Based on the text we can inference that the family member speaking is probably the mother of the teenagers.

USER_12.question: Re-read lines 6-8. Which of the following inferences is best supported by these lines?

responses.content: What she means by saying the line ”we become strangers in our own home.” She probably refers to the kids getting farther and farther away from their parents.
The “we” refer to the mother and the teenagers.

They say “we” because it’s referring to to everyone in the household. By saying this the mother means that every year every month and every week the teenagers get farther and farther away from their parents.

USER_12.question: Re-read lines 9-14. These lines primarily suggest that _____.

responses.content: The word code refers to the children personality traits and behaviors.
The code refers to gossiping and drama.
The teenagers don’t want the mom to hear the conversation.

USER_12.question: Write two or three sentences that explain what happens to the “strangers” when “years later, the door opens”?

responses.content: What happens to the teenagers when years later the doors open is that they’re now fully grown adults. What the mother means by “I see  familiar skin now stretched on long bodies”is that the children are now more mature converting them into adults.
